vendors.name: Oracle Aconex
vendors.tagline: Document control & project collaboration for mega-projects
vendors.market_position: The document-control standard on large Qatar projects (Oracle runs a .qa site). Used across the megaproject pipeline for collaboration and the common data environment.
pros: Deep document, RFI and submittal control
Neutral, auditable record across all parties
vendors.qa_specific_note: A collaboration backbone for large contractors and PMCs, not a back-office tool. Qatar has no VAT, so invoicing/tax is handled separately by your ERP.

vendors.name: Oracle Primavera P6
vendors.tagline: Critical-path scheduling & cost control for large projects
vendors.market_position: The scheduling and project-controls standard in Qatar — Ashghal (the Public Works Authority) is a named Oracle reference user, running Primavera P6 + Unifier across its infrastructure programme.
pros: Often mandated by government / EPC clients
vendors.qa_specific_note: On Ashghal and large public work, Primavera-format programmes are effectively required — plan for it from the bid stage.

vendors.name: Autodesk Construction Cloud
vendors.market_position: Available in Qatar through Autodesk Gold Partners in Doha (e.g. GIC Middle East, Thasaamah), with named local users among Qatari engineering firms. The BIM standard for design and coordination.
cons: BIM depth wasted on simple jobs
vendors.qa_specific_note: Bought and supported through Doha-based Autodesk partners rather than a direct Qatar office.

vendors.name: Procore
vendors.market_position: A strong global construction platform sold into Qatar from Procore’s Dubai MENA hub — there is no confirmed Qatar office, so support is regional.
vendors.qa_specific_note: Procore serves Qatar regionally from Dubai; confirm local implementation support during evaluation.

vendors.name: PlanRadar
vendors.market_position: A capable, Arabic-supported site-execution tool marketed across the GCC. Its Qatar-specific deployment wasn’t independently confirmed, but it’s a strong fit for snagging and inspections.
pros: Affordable per-user model with a free trial
Strong for snagging, QA and handover
vendors.typical_pricing: Per-user subscription (global USD card); Qatar pricing on request.
vendors.qa_specific_note: Available regionally; we couldn’t confirm a named Qatar deployment from a primary source — trial it for your own projects.

vendors.name: Focus Softnet
vendors.market_position: One of the few regional ERP houses with a real Doha office, covering construction ERP and CAFM for Qatari contractors and FM firms.
cons: Broad suite — more than small firms need
vendors.qa_specific_note: A genuine local presence in Doha is a real advantage for support — worth shortlisting for ERP/CAFM in Qatar.

vendors.name: PACT ERP
vendors.market_position: A regional contracting ERP (BOQ, estimation, subcontractor and project costing) with a Qatar branch and localised GCC support.
cons: Smaller ecosystem than the global tools
vendors.qa_specific_note: Construction-specific ERP with stated Qatar presence — good for contractors that want project costing plus accounting.

vendors.name: SkyStruct
vendors.market_position: The one clearly Qatar-built construction-PM product, headquartered at Qatar Science & Technology Park in Doha — cost management, activity/document tracking and in-app collaboration.
pros: Cost, activity, document and inventory tracking
vendors.qa_specific_note: The local-champion pick: a Qatar-native alternative to the global platforms, worth a look for Qatari contractors who value local product and support.

vendors.name: SWG
vendors.market_position: A CAFM and PPP-performance platform with a dedicated Qatar operation, used by facilities-management firms managing large building portfolios.
pros: Strong for FM and PPP-performance contracts
vendors.qa_specific_note: Most relevant for FM and maintenance contractors in Qatar rather than builders.

vendors.name: Insight CAFM
vendors.market_position: A CAFM tool purpose-built for the GCC need of managing labour camps and staff accommodation — a real pain point for large Qatari contractors.
pros: Purpose-built for GCC labour-camp / accommodation FM
Covers a real Gulf contractor need
vendors.qa_specific_note: A niche but genuinely useful pick if your Qatar operation runs worker accommodation at scale.

vendors.name: Monaqasat (Govt Procurement)
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s unified state-procurement portal (under the Ministry of Finance). It issues the contractor/supplier classification certificate that is mandatory to bid on government tenders.
cons: Classification required to bid public work
Needs audited accounts + named QA/QC heads
vendors.qa_specific_note: Qatar’s analogue to Saudi contractor classification — clean, audited financials from your ERP make classification and bidding far smoother.

vendors.name: General Tax Authority (GTA)
vendors.tagline: National tax authority — and no VAT (yet)
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s tax authority runs income, withholding and excise tax through the Dhareeba portal — but, unlike Saudi, levies no VAT today.
pros: No VAT currently levied in Qatar
cons: Withholding tax applies to some payments
Excise tax applies to certain goods
vendors.qa_specific_note: The biggest software contrast with Saudi: Qatar construction billing does NOT need a VAT/e-invoicing engine today — though a ~5% VAT is expected eventually.

vendors.name: Qatar E-Invoicing (draft)
vendors.tagline: Draft e-invoicing law — approved, not yet in force
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s Council of Ministers approved a draft e-invoicing law in May 2026, expected to follow the regional clearance model — but no mandatory date is set.
pros: Expected to mirror a Peppol-style model
vendors.qa_specific_note: Build for it, don’t gate on it — as of mid-2026 e-invoicing is not required in Qatar.

vendors.name: Qatarization (Law No. 12 of 2024)
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s 2024 Qatarization law obliges private firms to prioritise hiring and training Qatari nationals, with incentives for meeting quotas.
vendors.qa_specific_note: HR/payroll software used in Qatar should track Qatari national ratios — a different ruleset from both Saudi Nitaqat and UAE Emiratisation.

vendors.name: Ministry of Municipality (Building Permits)
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s building-permit authority, now running an AI-powered system that integrates with GIS, Ashghal and Kahramaa to cut permit times dramatically.
pros: Now an AI system (~120 min vs 30 days)
Integrated with GIS, Ashghal and Kahramaa
cons: Must conform to Qatar Construction Specifications
vendors.qa_specific_note: Permit-ready, QCS-compliant documentation speeds approvals — organised project documents pay off here.

vendors.name: Ashghal & Qatar Construction Specifications
vendors.tagline: The QCS 2014 — Qatar’s de-facto construction code
vendors.market_position: The Public Works Authority (Ashghal) maintains the Qatar Construction Specifications (QCS 2014) — the de-facto national building/construction code — and is a major infrastructure client.
pros: QCS 2014 is the national construction standard
Covers 22 sections incl. H&S and green building
cons: Specs/compliance tooling must map to QCS
vendors.qa_specific_note: QCS replaces international codes in Qatar — design and QA tooling should map to QCS sections, not US/UK codes.

vendors.name: Kahramaa (Electricity & Water)
vendors.tagline: Accredits contractors for electrical & water connections
vendors.market_position: Qatar’s sole electricity and water operator accredits and registers the contractors and consultants allowed to carry out electrical and water connection work.
vendors.qa_specific_note: MEP and utility contractors must hold Kahramaa accreditation to legally connect — factor it into your compliance checklist.
